registries.description: Peace is the condition of wholeness, right relationship, and rest — the Hebrew shalom covering far more than the absence of conflict. It means completeness, prosperity, welfare, the flourishing of everything and everyone in their proper place. It is used of the end of hostilities, of the quality of a life well-lived, of the relationship between a person and God when that relationship is restored. The New Testament declares that Christ himself is our peace: through him the fundamental rupture between humanity and God is healed. Peace is the signature of the kingdom.

registries.notes: COVERED — Exclusion validated 2026-04-06. Personality as a modern psychological construct — the distinctive pattern of traits that makes each person uniquely themselves — has no single biblical lexical anchor. Scripture addresses the formed character of the inner person through the anthropological core vocabulary: the soul (nephesh/psychē) as the living person in totality, the heart (lev/kardia) as the centre of thought, will, and emotion, the spirit (ruach/pneuma) as the person's God-ward dimension, and the conscience as the moral self-awareness before God. The totality of inner being expressed through these entries encompasses what personality means in the biblical framework, and the biblical account treats personhood as given and known by God before it is described by the person themselves. Covered by soul (#182), heart (#183), spirit (#184), conscience (#26). No further action required. Source: Session-A-v9-Empty-Words-Analysis-20260318.
registries.description: Personality — the distinctive pattern of traits, tendencies, and ways of being that makes each person uniquely themselves — does not have a dedicated biblical term. The concept is distributed across the vocabulary for character, heart, and the unique naming of each person by God. The biblical account treats personhood as given and known by God before it is described or understood by the person themselves. [Covered by soul (#182), heart (#183), spirit (#184).]
registries.inference_note: Personality as a psychological construct does not exist in biblical vocabulary. The concept is ABSENT as a term. Scripture addresses the formed character of the inner person through related but distinct terms.

registries.notes: REPLACED — Exclusion validated 2026-04-06. Personhood as a philosophical concept was replaced by being (#211) as the most fundamental biblical category. Being — the bare fact of existing, of having reality — underlies everything else in the corpus. The biblical vocabulary distributes this across soul (nephesh, the living being), spirit (ruach, the animated being), and the basic ontological vocabulary. The replacement moves from a philosophical construct to the programme's deepest anthropological category. Replacement registry #211 is active and Complete (15 groups confirmed). No further action required.
registries.description: Personhood — the status of being a person, with the dignity, relationality, and moral standing that status entails — is addressed in Scripture through the vocabulary of being, image, name, and calling rather than through a single term. To be a person is to be known by God, addressed by God, and accountable to God. Personhood is not a human achievement or a property that can be measured; it is given in the act of creation. [Replaced by being (#211).]

Perverseness is the state of being deliberately crooked — morally twisted away from what is right, not by ignorance but by inclination. The Hebrew vocabulary here carries the image of something bent out of its natural shape, of the path that deviates from the straight way. Perverseness is treated as a settled condition rather than a single act: the perverse person has a habitual orientation away from what is good and true, and their speech and conduct reflect this inner twist.

Praise is the outward expression of one's highest valuation of what is genuinely great and good. The Hebrew vocabulary is exceptional in its breadth: to shine (hallel), to give thanks with outstretched hands (yadah), to sing (zamar), to shout (rua), to boast in the best sense. Praise is directed toward God because he alone is fully worthy of it — the one whose greatness exceeds every other greatness. In Scripture, praise is not merely devotional expression; it is the confession of reality: God is what he is, and praise is the acknowledgement of that.

Prayer is the act of addressing God directly — speaking and listening within the relationship between the creature and the Creator. The Hebrew and Greek vocabulary covers petition, supplication, intercession, praise, and the casting of one's cares. Prayer is the breath of the inner life in relationship with God: it is how trust is exercised, how need is expressed, how grief is given language, and how the person remains in living contact with the One who made them. It assumes that God hears, that he is personal, and that the relationship is real.

registries.description: Pride is the inner conviction of one's own superiority — the heart that refuses to occupy its proper place as creature before Creator and equal before equal. The Hebrew vocabulary is extensive: arrogance, haughtiness, the lifting up of oneself, the refusal to bow. Scripture treats pride as the foundational human disorder — the sin beneath sins — because it is the root from which the refusal to trust and obey God grows. God opposes the proud. Yet the same vocabulary is used positively for the legitimate confidence of those who know who they are in God.

Prophecy is the speaking of what God has given one to say — the communication of divine perspective, intention, or truth through a human voice. The Hebrew and Greek vocabulary covers both the foretelling of what will happen and the forthtelling of what God is saying now. In the biblical understanding, prophecy is not primarily a human capacity but a gift and a calling: the prophet speaks because they have been given words to speak, not because they are particularly clever or insightful. The Word of God comes through the prophet, not from them.

Purity is the quality of being unmixed — single, uncontaminated, devoted to one thing without internal division or compromise. The Hebrew and Greek vocabulary covers both the ritual purity required for approach to God and the moral-inner purity that makes a person's motives and desires clean. Jesus radically internalises purity: blessed are the pure in heart. Purity of heart in the biblical sense is not innocence of experience but the absence of duplicity — wanting one thing, being directed toward one end.

registries.description: Purpose is the settled intention behind action — the why that gives direction and meaning to what one does. The Hebrew and Greek vocabulary is broad, covering purposes of the mind, the deliberate plans of both God and humans, and the underlying reason for existence. In Scripture, God's purpose is the bedrock of reality: what he intends comes to pass. Human purpose finds its proper form in alignment with divine purpose: not the imposition of one's own agenda on the world but the discovery and embrace of the purpose for which one was made.

Reasoning is the capacity to think through a problem — to work with ideas, weigh evidence, and arrive at conclusions through a process of deliberate reflection. The Greek vocabulary here covers both the process of disputing and the content of the reasoning itself. Scripture does not oppose reason; the prophets and Jesus and Paul all appeal to it. But reason in the biblical understanding is a dependent faculty: it functions rightly when it is operating within a framework of trust in God, and it goes wrong when it mistakes itself for the final arbiter of truth.

Rebellion is the wilful rejection of legitimate authority — the active refusal to be governed by one who has the right to govern. The Hebrew vocabulary is vivid: revolt, transgression, the active pushing against constraint. Rebellion against God is treated as the core of sin in the prophetic literature: Israel's history is narrated as a long pattern of departing from God and returning. Rebellion is not confusion or weakness but deliberate opposition. It is the posture of the person who knows the command and refuses it.

Recognition is the moment of seeing clearly — knowing someone or something for what they truly are, often after a period of confusion, distance, or blindness. The Greek vocabulary here (epiginōskō) carries the sense of coming to full, accurate knowledge of something, of the fog clearing. In the New Testament it often describes the recognising of Jesus for who he is: the disciples' eyes are opened, they recognise him. Recognition is the inner experience of truth breaking through — and it carries with it the responsibility to act on what has been seen.
registries.inference_note: Recognition — the inner act of perceiving and identifying something or someone correctly — is ABSENT as a single biblical term. The concept appears in knowledge, acknowledgment, and discernment vocabulary.

registries.description: Rejection is the experience of being turned away — refused, excluded, deemed unworthy or unwanted by someone whose acceptance mattered. The Greek term here (aposterēsis) covers the deprivation of what one was owed or expected. Rejection in Scripture is both an experience Jesus himself undergoes (he was despised and rejected) and a dynamic at the heart of the human-God relationship: the story of Israel and of humanity is in part the story of rejecting the One who comes to them. The one rejected by humans is vindicated by God.
registries.inference_note: Rejection as a psychological inner state — the felt experience of being unwanted or cast away — is ABSENT as a single biblical term. Scripture addresses rejection primarily from the perspective of being rejected by God or by people, with some texts addressing the inner experience.

registries.description: Rejoicing is the expressive, outward dimension of joy — the breaking out of inner gladness into movement, sound, and celebration. The Hebrew vocabulary here covers laughter, exultation, and the shout of joy. Rejoicing in Scripture is not a private matter; it tends to be communal and visible. The command to rejoice in the Lord is not an instruction to generate a feeling but to express what is genuinely true: that God is good, that his purposes stand, and that his people are in right relationship with him. Rejoicing is the form joy takes when it cannot be contained.

registries.notes: COVERED — Exclusion validated 2026-04-06. Reliability is faithfulness made concrete in repeated action — the quality of being trustworthy over time. H0529 (emun, faithfulness/reliability) and H0571 (emet, truth/reliability/steadfastness) are both confirmed within the faithfulness registry. No distinct lexical separation exists between reliability and faithfulness in the Hebrew emet/emunah family: reliability is not a distinct inner capacity but the observable behavioural expression of the inner quality of faithfulness. Covered by faithfulness (#60). No further action required. Source: Session-A-v9-Empty-Words-Analysis-20260318.
registries.description: Reliability is the quality of being trustworthy over time — doing what one has committed to do, being what one has claimed to be, consistently and without need for constant verification. The Hebrew vocabulary for faithfulness (emeth, emunah) covers this fully: reliability is faithfulness made concrete in action. A reliable person is one whose word is their bond and whose track record makes them safe to depend on. [Covered by faithfulness (#60).]

Renewal is the process of being made new again — not patched or improved but genuinely refreshed and restored to something better than the deteriorated state. The Hebrew and Greek vocabulary here covers change, the putting on of what is fresh, the process of interior renewal. The New Testament is particularly concerned with the renewal of the mind (Romans 12:2): the transformation of the inner faculty of understanding so that it perceives reality differently. Renewal is both a divine act and a human participation in what God is doing.

registries.description: Repentance is the turning — the change of direction in the inner person that results in a different way of living. The Hebrew vocabulary (shuv, nacham) covers returning, relenting, and the change of mind that leads to action; the Greek (metanoia) means a complete change of the inner orientation. Repentance is not guilt or sorrow alone, though both may accompany it; it is the actual turning from one direction and moving in another. God repents (changes his response) in the sense of relenting; human repentance is the return to God from a life turned away from him.

registries.notes: REPLACED — Exclusion validated 2026-04-06. Resentment was re-registered under #205 with a more precise lexical anchor targeting the slow-burning, grievance-holding dimension of anger. The inner-being reality of resentment — the refusal to release anger, the nursing of wrongs, the keeping of account — is distinct from acute anger or wrath and warranted its own entry. Replacement registry #205 is active and Complete as a pure XREF registry (all terms cross-reference to anger and related vocabulary owned in other registries, vc_groups=0 is correct). The dimension index will be populated through the OWNER registries during Session B. No further action required.
registries.description: Resentment is the smouldering inner anger that refuses to be released — the ongoing nursing of grievance against someone who has caused harm. It differs from grief in that it is directed outward at the person responsible; it differs from healthy anger in that it refuses resolution. The biblical vocabulary for this concept is embedded in the broader words for anger and bitterness. Resentment has a particularly corrosive quality: it harms the one who holds it as much as or more than its target. [Replaced by resentment (#205).]

Resolve is the inner quality of having decided and committed to a course of action — the settled will that does not waver once it has determined its direction. The Greek vocabulary here (eudokia, goodwill or good pleasure) covers the settled determination to act well rather than mere stubbornness. Resolve in the positive sense is the firm decision made in alignment with what is right and good. Paul uses this vocabulary to describe God's own settled, purposeful will: the good pleasure of his will. [XREF: covered by desire (#43).]

Reverence is the deep respect that arises in the presence of what is genuinely great, sacred, or holy — the inner disposition of the person who has recognised that they are in the presence of something that transcends them. The Hebrew vocabulary here (yare, to fear) covers the reverent fear that is the proper response to God. Reverence is closely related to awe but is more sustained: it is not just the momentary arrest of encounter but the ongoing posture of one who lives with a sense of the sacred. It is the foundation of genuine worship.

Righteousness is the quality of being in right relationship — with God, with others, with the created order — in a way that is measurably just and true. The Hebrew (tsedaqah) and Greek (dikaiosunē) vocabulary covers both the moral quality of the righteous person and the forensic status of those declared righteous by God. Righteousness is not primarily an inner quality one cultivates but a relational status: to be righteous is to be in right standing, to be in the proper relationship of creature to Creator and person to person.

Seeking is the sustained orientation of the inner person toward something not yet found — the purposeful reaching toward God, toward truth, toward what one needs. The Hebrew vocabulary (darash, baqash) covers both the diligent searching for something and the ongoing directional movement toward it. To seek God is not merely to pray or to read Scripture; it is the habitual, sustained turning of the inner life toward him as the source of what one most needs. Seeking is one of the fundamental postures of the life of faith.

registries.notes: COVERED — Exclusion validated 2026-04-06. Self-awareness as a modern psychological category — the capacity to see one's own inner states, motivations, and blindspots accurately — has no single biblical lexical anchor. The reflexive use of H3045 (yada, to know) covers self-knowledge in the Hebrew text, and Greek syneidēsis (conscience) is precisely the faculty of self-awareness before God: knowing one's own inner moral condition and standing. The examined life is not the final goal in Scripture, but Scripture does address the person who knows themselves — their guilt, their capacity, their position before God — through the conscience and knowledge vocabulary. Covered by conscience (#26), knowledge (#100). No further action required. Source: Session-A-v9-Empty-Words-Analysis-20260318.
registries.description: Self-awareness is the capacity to see oneself accurately — to know one's own inner states, motivations, tendencies, and blindspots as they actually are rather than as one would like them to be. The biblical vocabulary does not give this a single term but distributes it across conscience, knowledge, and the discernment that comes from walking with God. The examined life is not the final goal in Scripture, but the person who is self-aware is more capable of genuine repentance, humility, and growth than the person who is opaque to themselves. [Covered by conscience (#26).]

Self-control is the inner capacity to govern oneself — to choose what one does with one's appetites, impulses, and reactions rather than being governed by them. The Greek vocabulary here (egkrateia, sōphrosynē) covers the ability to hold back, the soundness of mind that keeps the whole person in good order. Self-control is listed as a fruit of the Spirit: it is not primarily the product of effort but of the Spirit's work reshaping the inner person. Without it, strength, intelligence, and desire all become dangerous rather than good.

registries.notes: PARTIAL — Research required. Partially covered by compassion (#23). Hebrew H7356 (rachamim, compassion/tender mercies) and G4698 (splagchnon, compassion as visceral inner sensitivity) are in the compassion semantic field. Recommend confirming compassion (#23) explicitly includes G4698 (11 occurrences) as the inner-sensitivity dimension. Source: Session-A-v9-Empty-Words-Analysis-20260318.
registries.description: Sensitivity is the quality of being genuinely responsive to others — attuned to their feelings, needs, and state in a way that makes one a good and caring presence. The biblical vocabulary does not isolate this as a distinct inner state but distributes it across compassion, gentleness, and the attentiveness of love. The person of genuine sensitivity is one in whom the image of God's own attentiveness to the creature is reflected. [Partially covered by compassion (#23).]
registries.inference_note: Sensitivity as a psychological construct — heightened responsiveness to emotional or spiritual stimuli — is ABSENT as a biblical term. The concept appears in Scripture through tender-heartedness, spiritual perception, and the condition of the conscience.

registries.description: Sensuality is the dimension of human experience that is directly tied to bodily sensation and pleasure — the realm of taste, touch, smell, beauty, and physical delight. The Greek vocabulary here (aselgeia, debauchery) tends to describe the disordered form of this; the wider biblical picture includes a positive account of created pleasure (the Song of Solomon, the goodness of creation). Sensuality is not inherently sinful but becomes disordered when it displaces other goods or pursues pleasure as an end in itself. [XREF: covered by flesh (#185).]

registries.notes: SESSION D POINTER — Researcher decision 2026-04-06. Reclassified from PARTIAL. Sexuality as a modern category has no single biblical lexical anchor (inference_note confirmed at Session A). The positive, created dimension of human sexuality is distributed across existing registries: flesh (#185, basar — embodied creatureliness), desire (#43, somatic dimension), covenant (#34), love (#103), and union/faithfulness vocabulary. The inner-being significance of sexual identity as a gendered being before God is a cross-registry synthesis question, not a single-term analysis question. No Session A run required. Session D synthesis should address: how the programme's data across flesh, desire, covenant, love, and unity together articulate the inner-being reality of sexuality as Scripture presents it. Source: Session-A-v9-Empty-Words-Analysis-20260318 (original); researcher decision 2026-04-06.
registries.description: Sexuality is the dimension of human identity and experience bound up with being male or female — the embodied reality of sexual differentiation, attraction, and union. The biblical vocabulary distributes this across the words for flesh (basar), for the marriage bond, for desire, and for the specific words that address sexual sin. Scripture is neither prudish nor permissive about sexuality: it takes it seriously as a fundamental human reality, grounds it in creation, protects it with law, and addresses its disorders without shame. [Partially covered by sensuality (#144), lust (#105).]
registries.inference_note: Sexuality as a broad category covering sexual identity, orientation, and expression is a modern construct ABSENT as a single biblical term. Scripture addresses specific sexual acts, desires, and relational covenants rather than sexuality as a category.

registries.description: Shame is the painful inner awareness of being exposed as inadequate, wrong, or unworthy — and the accompanying fear of rejection or contempt. The Hebrew vocabulary is extensive and connected to honour and disgrace: to be shamed is to be publicly diminished, stripped of one's standing. The body is fully implicated: the face falls or is covered, the person wants to disappear. God is said to put to shame those who oppose his people, and to honour those who hope in him. Shame is addressed most directly in the gospel: the one who trusts in him will not be put to shame.

Sin is the most comprehensive word for moral failure in Scripture — covering the full range of actions, attitudes, and conditions that fall short of or actively oppose what God intends. The Hebrew vocabulary spans missing the mark (chata), transgressing a boundary (pesha), twisting what is right (avon), and simply being evil (ra). Sin is not only acts but a condition: Paul describes humanity as being in sin as in a domain of power. The New Testament's great claim is that this condition is addressed by Christ, who takes sin's consequence and breaks its power.

Sincerity is the quality of being genuinely what one appears to be — without hidden motives, without the wearing of a face that conceals a different interior. The Greek vocabulary (eilikrineia) covers the testing of something pure in sunlight, the unmixed quality. Sincerity is the inner correspondence between what one says and what one actually means, between the face one presents and the person one actually is. It is closely related to integrity and honesty, but where integrity emphasises consistency over time, sincerity emphasises the unmixed quality of the present moment.

Slander is the deliberate damaging of a person's reputation through false or distorted speech. The Hebrew and Greek vocabulary is rich: blasphemy, reviling, the malicious tongue, the false witness. Slander is treated as a relational weapon: it wounds from a distance, using the power of words to destroy what someone has built. In the New Testament, slander against fellow believers is treated as a serious failure: it expresses contempt for the person God has accepted. Slander of God is blasphemy, the misuse of speech against its maker.

Sorcery is the attempt to access and use spiritual power through means other than the revealed and rightful path of relationship with God — through rituals, substances, or alliances with beings other than God. The Hebrew and Greek vocabulary covers both the practice itself and its practitioners. Scripture is consistently and strongly opposed to sorcery throughout both Testaments. The opposition is not because spiritual power is unreal but because accessing it through these means involves alliance with spiritual forces that are opposed to God and destructive to the person who seeks them.

registries.description: Sorrow is the deep inner pain of loss, grief, and distress — the weight of what has gone wrong or been lost. The Hebrew vocabulary here covers the heaviness of grief, the sickness of loss, the dismay of the person who cannot see a way forward. The New Testament introduces the concept of godly sorrow (2 Corinthians 7:10): the grief that turns a person toward God and leads to repentance, as distinct from worldly sorrow that simply produces death. Sorrow in Scripture is not to be suppressed; it is to be brought before God.

Strife is the condition of ongoing quarrel, conflict, and contention between people — the state where relationship has broken down into active hostility or persistent friction. The Hebrew and Greek vocabulary covers both the immediate quarrel and the underlying provocation that keeps conflict alive. Strife is treated as a social pathology: it destroys community, poisons relationships, and reflects the disordered inner states of those who generate it. The peaceable person is not one who never disagrees but one who does not stir up or maintain strife.

Stubbornness is the refusal to be moved — the persistence of a chosen position or direction in the face of contrary evidence, wise counsel, or God's own appeal to change. The Hebrew vocabulary here captures the hardened, stiff quality: the stiff neck is the image of the person who refuses to turn. Stubbornness is different from faithful endurance: endurance holds to what is right under pressure, while stubbornness holds to what is wrong under appeal. Israel's stubbornness toward God is one of the central themes of the prophetic literature.

registries.description: Stupor is a deprecated entry in this registry — the data originally filed under this word was correctly a study of sorrow, and has been fully transferred to that registry (#151). The word stupor itself, as the numbness and dullness of inner perception produced by overwhelming experience or judgment, is addressed within the broader vocabulary of spiritual blindness and hardness of heart. [Deprecated — data transferred to sorrow (#151).]
registries.inference_note: Spiritual stupor — a supernaturally induced or sin-produced dullness of spiritual perception — is ABSENT as a modern psychological term in Scripture. The concept appears in specific passages describing a spirit or sleep of deep insensibility.

registries.description: Submission is the voluntary placing of oneself under the authority of another — not enforced compliance but the willing acknowledgement of a proper ordering of relationship. The Greek vocabulary here is precise: to arrange oneself under, the military and relational sense of taking one's proper place in a structure of authority. Submission in Scripture is always contextual: it flows from love and wisdom rather than from domination. The supreme model is Christ's submission to the Father, which is the ground and example of all other submission.

Surrender is the giving up of self-governance — the inner act of letting go of one's claim to control one's own life, resources, or outcome. The Hebrew vocabulary here is built around the root for closing, shutting, and the coming out that happens when a city falls to a siege: surrender is what happens when resistance ends. In Scripture, surrender to God is not defeat but the beginning of genuine life: the self that is surrendered to God is not destroyed but restored. The theology of surrender runs through the entire biblical account of what it means to trust.
registries.inference_note: Surrender as a spiritual concept — the yielding of the will to God — is ABSENT as a single biblical term. The concept is expressed through several yielding and presenting terms.

registries.description: Temptation is the enticement toward what is wrong — the pull toward an action, attitude, or choice that would harm oneself, others, or one's relationship with God. The Greek vocabulary covers both the tempting and the testing: the same word is used for the trial that strengthens and the enticement that seeks to lead astray. Jesus is tempted in every way that humans are, yet without sin. Temptation is not sin: it is the moment before the choice. Scripture treats it as an unavoidable feature of creaturely life and promises a way through it.

Terror is the most extreme form of fear — the inner state produced by a threat so overwhelming that it shatters the capacity for rational response. The Hebrew vocabulary here is exceptionally rich: terror, trembling, dismay, shuddering, and the particular phrase terror on every side that appears in Jeremiah. Terror has a theological dimension: the Day of the Lord is described in terror vocabulary, and the enemies of God's people are struck with terror before them. It is the appropriate response of the creature to unconstrained power.

Testimony is the giving of witness — the public, verbal declaration of what one has seen, experienced, or knows to be true. The Hebrew and Greek vocabulary covers both the formal witness in a legal setting and the personal declaration of what God has done. In the New Testament, testimony is one of the primary modes of gospel proclamation: the witness tells what they have seen and heard. Testimony is personal: it is the one who was there saying this is what happened. It connects inner experience to public truth.

registries.description: Thought is the interior movement of the mind across ideas, memories, plans, and perceptions — the inner speech of the person reflecting on their world. The Hebrew and Greek vocabulary here is among the broadest in the corpus: saying to oneself, devising, planning, resembling, governing, reckoning. Thought in Scripture is morally freighted: the thought life is where intentions form, where desires take shape, where the fundamental orientation of the person is decided. The renewal of the mind is the renewal of thought.

registries.notes: REPLACED — Exclusion validated 2026-04-06. Transformation as an English label was re-registered under #202 with more precise lexical anchoring. The inner-being reality of transformation — genuine metamorphosis of the inner person, becoming different rather than merely behaving differently — is one of the most theologically significant inner-being categories in the New Testament (metamorphoō, Rom 12:2; 2 Cor 3:18; anakainōsis, renewal of the mind). The replacement grounds the concept in its specific biblical vocabulary. Replacement registry #202 is active and Complete (7 groups confirmed). No further action required.
registries.description: Transformation is the process of genuine inner change — not superficial adjustment or improved performance but the actual remaking of what a person is. The Greek vocabulary covers the metamorphosis, the change from one form to another; the Hebrew covers the crossing over from one condition to another. In the New Testament, transformation is the work of the Spirit conforming the believer to the image of Christ: a process that begins in this life and is completed in the resurrection. [Replaced by transformation (#202).]
registries.inference_note: Transformation as a general category is a modern abstraction. Scripture addresses specific transformation processes through distinct terms. The concept is ABSENT as a single term but richly present across multiple specific terms.

registries.description: Transgression is the crossing of a boundary — specifically a boundary that has been clearly marked by God or legitimate authority, which the transgressor knew was there and chose to cross. The Hebrew and Greek vocabulary covers both the act of crossing and the condition of having crossed: guilt, trespass, lawlessness. Transgression is wilful in a way that sin (which can be unintentional) is not: to transgress is to know the line and step over it. It is the vocabulary of deliberate moral violation.

registries.description: Trust is the reliance upon the reliability of another — the inner act of leaning on someone else's strength, faithfulness, or goodness rather than on one's own resources. The Hebrew vocabulary here covers resting upon, taking refuge, the confident security of the person who has found someone worthy to lean on. Trust is the inner side of faith: where faith is the orientation of the whole person, trust is the specific inner act of resting on what one has faith in. The entire psalmic tradition is shaped around the movement between the collapse and the renewal of trust.

Truthfulness is the commitment to correspondence between what one says and what is actually the case — the quality of the person whose word is reliable and whose account of things can be trusted. The Greek vocabulary here (alētheia) covers truth in the deepest sense: not just accurate information but the genuine, unveiled reality of things. Truthfulness is both a character quality (the truthful person) and a relational practice (speaking truth to one another). It is grounded in the character of God, who cannot lie.

Unbelief is the refusal to receive and act on what God has said — not merely intellectual doubt but the settled unwillingness to trust. The Greek vocabulary covers both disobedience and unbelief as a single cluster, which is theologically significant: to disbelieve is to disobey, and to disobey is to disbelieve. Unbelief is not presented in Scripture primarily as an intellectual failure (though it may involve one) but as a relational rejection: the person who does not believe God has not received what he has offered.

Understanding is the capacity to grasp what something means — to perceive not just the surface of a thing but its significance, its inner logic, its connection to everything else. The Hebrew vocabulary (binah, sakal) covers both the insight that perceives and the intelligent action that results from perceiving rightly. Understanding in Scripture is never merely cerebral: it leads to action, to the fear of the Lord, to the reshaping of how one lives. Spiritual understanding is a gift, but it is also cultivated through attentiveness to what God has revealed.

Unity is the condition of many becoming genuinely one — not mere proximity or agreement but the actual shared life of those who are bound together in something deeper than circumstance. The Greek vocabulary here covers the oneness of those who share the same Spirit and the harmony of those who are genuinely aligned. The New Testament is particularly insistent on unity within the community of faith: the prayer of Jesus in John 17 is that his people would be one as he and the Father are one. Unity is both a gift and a calling.

Uprightness is the quality of the person who is straight — morally and relationally aligned with what is true and right, without the crooked turns of duplicity or self-interest. The Hebrew vocabulary covers the plain, the level, the path that does not deviate. Uprightness is related to integrity and righteousness but has a particular flavour: it is the quality of the person who goes straight, who does not take the roundabout path of manipulation or self-deception. The upright person is one in whose inner and outer life there is no hidden bend.

registries.notes: REPLACED — Exclusion validated 2026-04-06. Vulnerability was originally listed as PARTIAL/GAP with no existing partial coverage identified. The gap was subsequently addressed by new registry #206 (vulnerability) anchored in H6168 (arah, to lay bare/expose), H6174 (arom, naked/exposed), and G1131 (gymnos, naked/vulnerable — including Heb 4:13 naked and laid bare before God). The inner-being dimension of vulnerability — genuine openness and exposure, the capacity to be wounded or known at depth, the person stripped of self-protection before God and others — is substantive and now fully represented. Replacement registry #206 is active, Complete, and the most substantive of all replacement registries (34 groups confirmed). No further action required.
registries.description: Vulnerability is the condition of being genuinely open — capable of being hurt, of being known at depth, of being affected by what happens. The Hebrew and Greek vocabulary here covers nakedness and uncovering: the state of being without the protective layers one normally carries. Scripture presents vulnerability as the necessary condition for genuine relationship: the person who cannot be wounded cannot really love or be loved. The exposure of the cross is the supreme image of divine vulnerability — God making himself open to rejection and suffering.
registries.inference_note: Vulnerability as a psychological concept — the state of being open to being hurt or the willing openness of authentic relationship — is ABSENT as a biblical term. The concept appears in Scripture through weakness, nakedness, and the openness before God.

registries.description: Weakness is the condition of being without sufficient strength to accomplish what is needed — the experience of one's own limitation and insufficiency. The Greek vocabulary here (astheneia) covers physical, moral, and spiritual weakness. In the New Testament, weakness is reframed rather than merely lamented: Paul's discovery is that God's power is most clearly visible in human weakness, that the strength that matters is not one's own. Weakness in this frame is not a problem to be solved but a condition to be inhabited honestly, which creates the space for divine strength.

Whoredom is the biblical vocabulary for unfaithfulness in the covenant — specifically the turning of devotion and service away from God toward other gods or other sources of security. The Hebrew root (zanah, fornication) is used metaphorically throughout the prophets to describe Israel's idolatry: the nation that was betrothed to God has pursued other lovers. The metaphor is chosen deliberately to capture the relational betrayal involved in idolatry — it is not merely rule-breaking but the breaking of a bond of love and fidelity.

Wickedness is the comprehensive term for the condition and conduct of the person who has fundamentally departed from what is right — living by what is harmful, unjust, and ungodly. The Hebrew and Greek vocabulary spans harmful, unjust, and morally corrupt. Wickedness in Scripture is not merely bad behaviour but a settled orientation of the inner person away from God: the wicked person thinks and wants and chooses wrongly as a matter of character rather than accident. It is the spiritual condition that sin, left unaddressed, produces.

registries.description: Will is the inner capacity to choose and act — the faculty by which a person decides what they want, what they will do, and whom they will serve. The Hebrew and Greek vocabulary here is the broadest in the entire corpus: heart, purpose, pleasure, deliberate action, the deep inclination of the person. Will in Scripture is neither the absolute self-determination of modern individualism nor the mere helplessness of determinism; it is the genuine but dependent freedom of the creature whose choices are real and whose character shapes those choices.

registries.description: Wisdom is the capacity to live rightly in the world — to see what is true, choose what is good, and act in a way that fits with how things actually are. The Hebrew vocabulary (chokhmah, binah, sakal) covers both the practical skill of living and the deeper understanding that comes from knowing God. Wisdom begins with the fear of the Lord: the properly ordered relationship with God that makes genuine perception and right action possible. God himself is described as wise; human wisdom is a participation in and reflection of his.

Wonder is the inner response of the person who has encountered something that exceeds their capacity to contain or explain — the experience of being undone and enlarged at the same time. The Hebrew and Greek vocabulary covers amazement, astonishment, the particular quality of being arrested and struck by what is greater than expected. Wonder in Scripture is not merely a pleasant aesthetic experience but a theological posture: it is the appropriate response to the works and ways of God, whose greatness and goodness continually exceed what could be anticipated.

Worship is the total orientation of the person toward God — expressed in posture, voice, action, and the ordering of the whole life around his worth. The Hebrew vocabulary covers bowing down (shachah) and the service or ministry (abad) that gives God what he is owed; the Greek adds the sense of doing homage. Worship is not merely what happens in a gathered service but the fundamental posture of the creature rightly relating to the Creator: giving God what he is worth, which is everything. It is the outer expression of the inner reality of love, trust, and reverence.

Worth is the intrinsic value of something — what it is actually worth, not what the market assigns to it or what flattery claims for it. The Hebrew vocabulary here covers both the likeness or comparison that establishes value and the formal valuation that sets a price. In Scripture, worth is ultimately theological: the worth of a human being derives from being made in the image of God and known by name. Worth is also relational: to be worthy of something is to be fittingly matched to it.

registries.description: Wrath is the settled, purposeful anger of God against everything that corrupts, destroys, and opposes his good creation — not a loss of temper but the moral seriousness of one who will not let evil go unaddressed forever. The Hebrew vocabulary here is spare but intense. Wrath in Scripture is not God's most fundamental attitude toward humanity (love is) but it is his uncompromising response to sin. The gospel addresses wrath directly: what is owed is met, what threatened is turned away, and the wrath that would have fallen is received by the Son.

Yearning is the deep, physical ache of longing for someone loved and absent — the Hebrew root here (racham, compassion) connects yearning to the tender, womb-like love that longs to hold and protect what it loves. It is one of the most intimate words in the emotional vocabulary: not just wanting but aching, the longing of someone who loves deeply for the one who is away. It appears in only one verse in this registry, but the depth of the word is evident: this is love made urgent by absence.

Yielding is the act of giving way — producing what is asked, bearing what is expected, surrendering what one has been holding. The Hebrew vocabulary here covers sowing and bearing: the agrarian image of the ground that yields its fruit, and the person who delivers or leads or pays what is owed. Yielding is closely related to surrender and submission but is more concrete: it is the actual handing over, the productive giving of what was asked, rather than the inner orientation that precedes it.

Zeal is the burning intensity of concern for something one loves — the passionate energy that will not be neutral or half-hearted in relation to what one values. The Greek vocabulary here (spoudē, diligence) captures the eager, earnest quality of zeal rather than its most extreme form. In Scripture, zeal for God is consistently commended, but it must be aligned with knowledge: Paul's warning is that zeal without right understanding is destructive (Romans 10:2). Zeal for the wrong things, or for the right things by wrong means, is one of the great dangers of the religious person.

registries.description: Soul (nephesh / psychē) is the most important single word in the entire corpus — the word that comes closest to meaning the living human person as a whole. The Hebrew nephesh originally meant throat or breath, then became the word for the appetite that drives a living creature, then expanded to mean the person's very life and self. The Greek psychē carries similar weight. When Jesus says whoever loses their life will find it, he uses this word. The soul is not a separate part of a person that can be detached; it is the whole person considered as a living, breathing, wanting being.

registries.description: Flesh (basar / sarx) is the word for the human person considered as embodied, creaturely, and mortal — made of matter, subject to hunger and pleasure and death. Flesh is not evil in itself but becomes disordered when it rules the person rather than being ordered under God.

registries.description: Malice is the deliberate desire to harm — the inner disposition that actively wishes ill on another and is pleased when they suffer. The Hebrew vocabulary here covers what is harmful and what is contemptible. Malice is distinguished from anger (which responds to wrong) by its pleasure in harm: it is not triggered by injury but is a settled hostile orientation toward others.

registries.description: Contempt is the inner dismissal of another as beneath consideration — the attitude that treats someone as worthless, ridiculous, or unworthy of respect. The Hebrew vocabulary covers to lighten, to despise, to taunt, to reject as of no account. Jesus's warning in the Sermon on the Mount treats contempt (calling someone a fool) with the same gravity as murder, because both proceed from the same inner dismissal of the other's worth.

Doubt in Scripture is often the companion of genuine faith rather than its opposite — the honest wrestling of a person who is trying to trust in the face of difficulty.

registries.description: Resentment is the slow-burning anger that refuses to be released — the holding of grievance over time, nursing the memory of wrongs done and keeping the account open. It differs from righteous anger (which seeks resolution) and from grief (which mourns without hostility). The biblical vocabulary for this concept is embedded in the anger and bitterness families. Resentment is spiritually corrosive: it poisons the person who holds it, keeps old wounds open, and prevents the renewal that forgiveness and reconciliation make possible.

registries.description: Vulnerability is the condition of being genuinely open and exposed — capable of being wounded, affected, or known at depth. The Hebrew vocabulary here is built on uncovering and nakedness: the state of the person who has no protective shell. In Scripture, vulnerability is the necessary condition for genuine love and genuine relationship: the person who cannot be hurt cannot really give themselves. The cross is the supreme image of divine vulnerability — God in Christ making himself open to rejection, suffering, and death. It is the form love takes when it is fully serious.
registries.inference_note: Vulnerability as a psychological concept — the state of being open to being hurt or the willing openness of authentic relationship — is ABSENT as a biblical term. The concept appears in Scripture through weakness, nakedness, and the openness before God.

registries.description: Blindness (spiritual) is the condition of the inner person whose capacity to perceive spiritual and moral reality has been impaired or closed — not the failure of physical sight but the failure of the deeper seeing by which one recognises God, truth, and what is real. The Greek vocabulary covers both darkening and the condition of blindness. Jesus heals physical blindness as a sign of the deeper healing he brings: he is the light of the world, and those who follow him will not walk in darkness. Spiritual blindness can be the result of judgment, of choice, or of deception.

registries.description: Sloth is the failure to invest the effort that responsibility and love require — the habitual preference for rest over work, for comfort over the demands of one's calling. The Hebrew and Greek vocabulary here covers sluggishness and the quality of going slack. Sloth is not mere tiredness but the settled orientation of the person who will not press through the friction of genuine engagement. It is treated in Scripture as a moral failure: the servant who buries his talent is the servant who preferred safety to the risk of genuine investment.

registries.description: Likeness (homoiōsis) is the Greek word that captures the quality of resemblance — being like, having the same form as. It appears alongside image in Genesis (made in our image and likeness) and in the New Testament in Paul's description of the transformation of believers into the likeness of Christ. Likeness is the relational and experiential dimension of the image: where image points to the formal representation, likeness points to the actual similarity that results from genuine relationship. To become like Christ is the goal of the transformation process.

registries.description: Deadness is the condition of what once lived being no longer alive — the absence of responsiveness, vitality, and capacity for relationship. The Hebrew and Greek vocabulary covers both physical death and the spiritual condition of being dead while alive. Paul uses deadness to describe humanity's condition apart from God: dead in trespasses and sins. The resurrection of Jesus is the reversal of deadness: not merely the resuscitation of what was dying but the restoration of genuine life, more fully alive than before.
registries.inference_note: Spiritual deadness — the condition of the human spirit being non-functional toward God — is ABSENT as a single biblical term. It is expressed through the language of death applied to the inner person.

registries.description: Being — the bare fact of existing, of having reality — is the deepest and most fundamental category of all, underlying everything else in the corpus. The biblical vocabulary for this distributes itself across soul (nephesh, the living being), spirit (ruach, the animated being), and the basic ontological affirmations of creation: God is, God causes to be, the creature is in relation to the Creator. To be is not taken for granted in Scripture: existence is given, sustained, and known by God. [XREF: covered by soul (#182).]
